cards.front: What are the main relationship types between objects covered in Chapter 23?

cards.text: A square {{c1::is-a}} shape, a car {{c2::has-a}} steering wheel, a programmer {{c3::uses-a}} keyboard, and a flower {{c4::depends-on}} a bee for pollination.

cards.front: What relationship type describes a 'part-of' relationship?
cards.back: Composition - where a part (member) is part of the whole object and the object manages the part's existence.

cards.text: A {{c1::has-a}} relationship is typically implemented using {{c2::aggregation}}, where the part can exist independently of the whole.

cards.front: Which object relationship type is explored through C++'s inheritance model and virtual functions?
cards.back: The is-a relationship, which is covered in the following two chapters after Chapter 23.

cards.concept: Why understanding object relationships is important in C++
cards.explanation:
Understanding object relationships helps:
  • Improve code reusability
  • Write more extensible classes
  • Apply real-world patterns to programming objects
  • Make better design decisions about class structure
examples: // Composition: Point is part of Shape
// Aggregation: Teacher has-a Student (but doesn't own)

cards.text: The process of building complex objects from simpler ones is called {{c1::object composition}}.

cards.back: Association relationship - where a class uses an otherwise unrelated object
Dependency relationship - where one class depends on another to perform a specific task

cards.front: How do real-life object relationships help us understand programming objects?
cards.back: Real-life relationships (like flowers and plants) help us recognize patterns that we can apply to programming objects, allowing us to infer behavior and structure from abstract concepts we already understand.

cards.text: The relationship verb for composition is {{c1::part-of}}, for aggregation is {{c2::has-a}}, for association is {{c3::uses-a}}, and for dependency is {{c4::depends-on}}.
